skill.name: auth
skill.description: Framework-neutral authentication guidance for JWT tokens, OAuth2, RBAC, MFA, API security middleware, and zero-trust access patterns.
skill.summary: Plan and review modern web authentication patterns across common application stacks.
skill.author: Azeem-2

security_audit.summary: The static command-execution and prompt-injection detections are false positives from markdown fences and role labels, not executable behavior. The skill is not malicious, but several authentication examples include risky guidance such as unused OAuth state validation, permissive CSP directives, plaintext backup-code handling, and audit logging that can expose authorization headers.

medium_findings.title: OAuth state parameter is accepted but not verified
medium_findings.description: The OAuth exchange methods accept a state value but do not compare it with a server-side stored state before exchanging the code. This weakens CSRF protection in the OAuth callback flow.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code includes a state parameter in both exchange methods, but no validation logic is shown before token exchange. This is a concrete OAuth security gap in example guidance.

medium_findings.title: Audit logging may expose authorization headers
medium_findings.description: The middleware logs the full request headers and only redacts exact keys such as password, token, secret, and key. Authorization and cookie headers can remain in logs and expose bearer tokens or sessions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example converts all headers into log data and the redaction list does not include authorization or cookie. The exposure path is direct and easy to verify.

medium_findings.title: MFA backup codes are generated as short plaintext values
medium_findings.description: The backup-code example creates six-digit numeric codes and verifies them from a plaintext list. Recovery codes should have more entropy, be stored hashed, and be consumed with audit controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code shows six-digit backup codes and direct list membership checks. The snippet does not hash or protect stored recovery codes.

medium_findings.title: Security header example permits unsafe script execution
medium_findings.description: The Content Security Policy allows unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval for scripts. Those directives weaken XSS defenses and conflict with strict production security guidance.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The CSP directives are explicitly present in the security middleware example. They are commonly unsafe unless a narrow compatibility exception is justified.

low_findings.title: Static command-execution detections are markdown false positives
low_findings.description: The external command detections correspond to markdown code fences around examples, not Ruby backtick execution or shell invocation. No evidence of executable shell commands was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are markdown fence delimiters. The surrounding content is static documentation and code examples, not shell execution logic.

low_findings.title: Prompt-injection detection is a role enum false positive
low_findings.description: The flagged role-token location is an enum member named USER inside an RBAC example. No instruction to override system behavior, skip review, or change evaluator policy was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of a Python enum-like role mapping. It does not contain prompt-injection language or a directive to the evaluator.

low_findings.title: Zero-trust example uses permissive placeholders
low_findings.description: The zero-trust example treats private networks as low risk and returns true for device trust as a placeholder. This is acceptable as illustrative material only, but unsafe if copied directly into production.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The placeholder comments make legitimate instructional intent likely, but the shown defaults would be weak if used without replacement.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive header logging pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The middleware builds log data from all request headers before redaction, which can preserve authorization and cookie secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code logs the headers dictionary and the redaction routine does not target common sensitive header names.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permissive CSP directives
dangerous_patterns.description: The example CSP includes unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval in script-src, reducing protection against injected scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The unsafe CSP keywords appear directly in the security middleware example.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext recovery-code verification
dangerous_patterns.description: The MFA example verifies backup codes by direct plaintext membership and removal from a list.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The recovery-code verification logic is explicit and no hashing or secure storage boundary is shown.

content.value_statement: Teams need practical authentication patterns that cover tokens, OAuth, roles, MFA, and API security. This skill provides framework-neutral templates and review checklists.
actual_capabilities: Explains JWT access and refresh token patterns with expiration and revocation concepts.
Provides OAuth2 provider examples for Google and GitHub sign-in flows.
Outlines role-based access control with roles, permissions, and decorators.
Covers MFA setup with TOTP, QR codes, backup codes, SMS, and email concepts.
Shows middleware patterns for security headers, rate limiting, and audit logging.
Includes a production checklist for authentication and security controls.
limitations: The examples are documentation snippets and are not a complete production library.
Several security examples need hardening before direct production use.
It does not provide framework-specific migrations, database schemas, or deployment automation.
OAuth, MFA, and logging patterns require review by an application security owner.

use_cases.title: Design a new sign-in system
use_cases.description: Map token lifetimes, refresh behavior, OAuth providers, and account recovery requirements before implementation.

use_cases.title: Review an existing auth flow
use_cases.description: Compare current code against common controls for MFA, RBAC, sessions, logging, and API protection.

use_cases.title: Create implementation tickets
use_cases.description: Turn broad authentication requirements into scoped engineering tasks with expected controls and review checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Plan basic authentication
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new web app.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the auth skill to outline a secure email and password sign-in flow for my web app. Include token lifetimes, password hashing, and session expiry.

prompt_templates.title: Add OAuth login
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate integration with third-party identity providers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the auth skill to design Google and GitHub OAuth login for my app. Include state validation, callback handling, account linking, and failure cases.

prompt_templates.title: Review API protection
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of authorization and middleware controls.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the auth skill to review my API security design. Check RBAC boundaries, rate limits, audit logging, token validation, and sensitive data handling.

prompt_templates.title: Create a production rollout plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced rollout across authentication, MFA, and monitoring.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the auth skill to create a production rollout plan for JWT, OAuth2, MFA, RBAC, and zero-trust checks. Include risks, tests, and rollback criteria.

output_examples.input: Plan authentication for a SaaS dashboard with admins and regular users.
output: Recommended a short-lived access token and rotating refresh token model.
Defined admin and user permission groups with ownership checks.
Flagged MFA as required for administrators and optional for regular users.

output_examples.input: Review an OAuth callback design before implementation.
output: Checked state validation, redirect URI matching, token exchange handling, and account-linking rules.
Identified logging and error-handling controls needed before launch.

output_examples.input: Prepare a security checklist for a production auth release.
output: Produced checks for token expiry, rate limiting, audit retention, MFA recovery, and secrets rotation.
Separated launch blockers from follow-up hardening tasks.

best_practices: Treat every snippet as a starting point and perform a security review before production use.
Use provider documentation and current framework guidance when implementing OAuth, MFA, and token storage.
Test authentication flows with abuse cases, expired credentials, replay attempts, and permission boundary checks.
anti_patterns: Do not copy example secrets, placeholder policies, or permissive defaults into production.
Do not log authorization headers, cookies, tokens, passwords, or recovery codes.
Do not skip OAuth state validation, redirect URI validation, or MFA recovery-code hashing.

faq.question: Is this skill a production-ready authentication library?
faq.answer: No. It is guidance with examples and checklists. Engineers must adapt, test, and harden each pattern.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it support?
faq.answer: The guidance is framework-neutral. Examples use Python-style patterns, but concepts apply across web stacks.

faq.question: Can it help with OAuth2 provider setup?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers provider URLs, code exchange concepts, user-info requests, and important validation steps.

faq.question: Does it cover authorization after login?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes RBAC concepts, permission checks, ownership checks, and protected endpoint patterns.

faq.question: Does it include MFA guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It discusses TOTP, QR setup, backup codes, SMS, email, and admin MFA requirements.

faq.question: What should teams review most carefully?
faq.answer: Review OAuth state handling, token storage, audit logging, CSP rules, backup-code storage, and placeholder zero-trust logic.
